imu-selection.title: MEMS IMU Selection Guide: How to Choose the Right Inertial Measurement Unit | MEMS-MCTI Tech Blog
imu-selection.description: Complete MEMS IMU selection guide: detailed explanation of key parameters like gyro bias stability, angular random walk, bandwidth, helping you choose the most suitable inertial measurement unit
imu-selection.keywords: MEMS IMU selection, IMU parameters, gyro bias stability, accelerometer selection, MEMS sensors
imu-selection.og_title: MEMS IMU Selection Guide: How to Choose the Right IMU
imu-selection.og_description: Detailed explanation of key parameters like gyro bias stability, angular random walk, and bandwidth to help you choose the best inertial measurement unit
imu-selection.h1: MEMS IMU Selection Guide: How to Choose the Right Inertial Measurement Unit
imu-selection.intro: Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) are core sensors in modern navigation systems, widely used in UAVs, aerospace, maritime navigation, autonomous driving, and industrial automation. This article details the key technical parameters to consider when selecting a MEMS IMU, helping engineers choose the most suitable product for their specific application needs.
imu-selection.p1_1: A typical MEMS IMU contains two core sensors:
imu-selection.li1_1: Three-axis gyroscope: Measures angular velocity around three orthogonal axes (°/s)
imu-selection.li1_2: Three-axis accelerometer: Measures linear acceleration along three orthogonal axes (m/s² or g)
imu-selection.p1_2: Some high-end IMUs may also integrate a three-axis magnetometer to provide absolute heading reference.
imu-selection.p2_1: Measurement range refers to the maximum angular velocity the gyroscope can measure. Common specifications range from ±300°/s to ±1000°/s. When selecting, consider the maximum angular velocity of the platform:
imu-selection.p2_2: Bias stability is the most critical indicator of IMU performance, representing the random drift of the gyroscope output signal under static conditions, usually expressed in °/h (degrees per hour). This parameter directly determines the long-term accuracy of attitude solution.
imu-selection.p2_3: The PA-IMU-01D's gyroscope bias stability is less than 0.5°/h, meaning the attitude error does not exceed 0.5 degrees per hour.
imu-selection.p2_4: Angular random walk is the integral effect of gyroscope output white noise, usually expressed in °/√h. It describes the rate at which gyroscope angle estimation error grows over time. This parameter is particularly important for applications requiring fast dynamic response.
imu-selection.p2_5: Bandwidth refers to the frequency range over which the gyroscope output can accurately follow the input angular velocity, expressed in Hz. The PA-IMU-01D's bandwidth exceeds 100Hz, meeting the dynamic response requirements of most control systems. Higher bandwidth is needed for high-speed UAVs or guided weapons.
imu-selection.p2_6: Scale factor non-linearity represents the degree of deviation from a linear relationship between gyroscope output and input angular velocity, usually expressed in ppm (parts per million). The PA-IMU-01D's scale factor non-linearity is less than 100ppm, ensuring high measurement accuracy across the full range.
imu-selection.p3_1: Accelerometer measurement range is usually expressed in g (gravitational acceleration). The PA-IMU-01D's accelerometer range is ±10g, capable of withstanding high shocks and vibrations. For bridge monitoring and other micro-vibration measurements, a smaller range (such as ±2g) may be needed for higher resolution.
imu-selection.p3_2: Accelerometer bias stability determines the accuracy of acceleration measurement under static conditions. The PA-IMU-01D's accelerometer bias stability is less than 1mg (milligrams), meaning it can distinguish acceleration changes of 0.001g.
imu-selection.p3_3: Resolution is the smallest acceleration change the accelerometer can distinguish. High resolution is critical for precision measurement applications such as structural health monitoring and seismic monitoring.
imu-selection.env1: Operating Temperature: PA-IMU-01D supports -40°C to +60°C operating temperature range
imu-selection.env2: Shock Resistance: High shock resistance design, capable of withstanding 1000g impact
imu-selection.env3: Vibration Resistance: Stable output in high-vibration environments
imu-selection.iface1: RS-422: Long-distance, strong anti-interference capability, suitable for industrial sites
imu-selection.iface3: CAN: Automotive and industrial control standard, supports high-speed communication
imu-selection.summary: Selecting the right MEMS IMU requires comprehensive consideration of accuracy requirements, environmental conditions, interface types, and budget. The PA-IMU series provides a complete product line from consumer-grade to industrial-grade, meeting the needs of most application scenarios.
imu-selection.contact: For further selection consultation, please contact our technical support team.
imu-selection.related1: IMU vs AHRS vs GPS-INS: Which Navigation System Do You Need?
imu-selection.related2: RTK GPS Principles: Achieving Centimeter-Level Positioning
imu-selection.cardTitle: MEMS IMU Selection Guide: How to Choose the Right Inertial Measurement Unit
imu-selection.cardExcerpt: This article details key technical parameters of MEMS inertial measurement units, including gyro bias stability, angular random walk, bandwidth and other core specifications, as well as application scenarios, environmental conditions and interface types to consider when selecting.

imu-vs-ahrs.title: IMU vs AHRS vs GPS-INS: Which Navigation System Do You Need? | MEMS-MCTI Tech Blog
imu-vs-ahrs.description: In-depth comparison of IMU, AHRS and GPS-INS: principles, characteristics and applications to help engineers choose the right navigation solution
imu-vs-ahrs.keywords: IMU vs AHRS, GPS-INS inertial navigation, navigation system selection, attitude reference system, integrated navigation
imu-vs-ahrs.og_title: IMU vs AHRS vs GPS-INS: Which Navigation System Do You Need?
imu-vs-ahrs.og_description: In-depth comparison of IMU, AHRS and GPS-INS: principles, characteristics and applications
imu-vs-ahrs.h1: IMU vs AHRS vs GPS-INS: Which Navigation System Do You Need?
imu-vs-ahrs.intro: In navigation system selection, engineers often face the choice between IMU, AHRS and GPS-INS. Each has its own characteristics and is suitable for different applications. This article provides an in-depth analysis of the principles, advantages, disadvantages and application scenarios of these three systems to help you make the right choice.
imu-vs-ahrs.p1_1: IMU is the raw sensor unit of a navigation system. Its core function is to measure the angular velocity and linear acceleration of the platform. It outputs raw sensor data without processing:
imu-vs-ahrs.p1_2: The IMU itself does not perform attitude solution; an external processor is required to convert raw data into attitude angles (pitch, roll, heading).
imu-vs-ahrs.p2_1: AHRS integrates a magnetometer and/or GPS on the IMU basis, with built-in attitude fusion algorithms, capable of directly outputting:
imu-vs-ahrs.p2_2: PA-AHRS01 also integrates a GPS receiver, capable of outputting latitude and longitude position information.
imu-vs-ahrs.p3_1: GPS-INS is the most advanced navigation system, deeply fusing GPS (or other satellite positioning systems) with INS (Inertial Navigation System), capable of providing:
imu-vs-ahrs.li_pros5: Suitable for deep integration with flight control systems
imu-vs-ahrs.li_cons2: Attitude error accumulates drift over time
imu-vs-ahrs.li_cons3: No absolute heading reference (magnetometer assistance required)
imu-vs-ahrs.li_cons4: Output frequency usually lower than raw IMU
imu-vs-ahrs.li_pros7: GPS and INS complement each other, highest accuracy
imu-vs-ahrs.li_pros8: INS maintains high accuracy when GPS fails
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen1: UAV flight control systems: Flight control needs raw sensor data for custom filtering and fusion
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen2: Robot attitude control: High-speed, low-latitude attitude feedback required
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen3: Industrial stabilization platforms: Need deep integration with existing control systems
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen4: Research institutions: Need to develop their own navigation algorithms
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen5: UAV complete products: Ready to use, shortened development cycle
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen6: Vehicle attitude monitoring: Need to directly read attitude angles
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen7: Marine attitude monitoring: Some attitude accuracy requirements
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen9: Aviation navigation: Complete position and attitude information required
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen10: High-precision surveying: Centimeter-level accuracy in RTK mode
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen11: Autonomous driving: High-precision position and attitude required
imu-vs-ahrs.li_scen12: Precision agriculture: Need precise operation position records
imu-vs-ahrs.summary: These three navigation systems serve different application needs:
imu-vs-ahrs.li_sum1: IMU: Suitable for applications requiring deep customization and control
imu-vs-ahrs.li_sum2: AHRS: Suitable for products requiring plug-and-play attitude output
imu-vs-ahrs.li_sum3: GPS-INS: Suitable for high-end applications requiring complete navigation solutions
imu-vs-ahrs.contact: When selecting, consider accuracy requirements, budget constraints, development cycle and technical capabilities. For further consultation, please contact our technical support team.
imu-vs-ahrs.cardTitle: IMU vs AHRS vs GPS-INS: Which Navigation System Do You Need?
imu-vs-ahrs.cardExcerpt: In-depth comparison of IMU, AHRS and GPS-INS: principles, characteristics and applications to help engineers choose the right navigation solution

rtk-gps.title: RTK GPS Principles: Key Technologies for Centimeter-Level Positioning | MEMS-MCTI Tech Blog
rtk-gps.description: RTK GPS principles explained: detailed introduction to carrier phase measurement, differential correction, ambiguity resolution and other core technologies for centimeter-level positioning
rtk-gps.keywords: RTK GPS principles, carrier phase positioning, differential GPS, centimeter-level positioning, GPS positioning accuracy, RTK surveying
rtk-gps.og_title: RTK GPS Principles: Key Technologies for Centimeter-Level Positioning
rtk-gps.og_description: Detailed introduction to RTK carrier phase measurement, differential correction, ambiguity resolution and other core technologies
rtk-gps.h1: RTK GPS Principles: Key Technologies for Centimeter-Level Positioning
rtk-gps.intro: RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) is a high-precision GPS positioning technology that can achieve centimeter-level positioning accuracy in real-time conditions. This article provides a detailed introduction to RTK working principles, key technologies and application scenarios.
rtk-gps.p1_1: GPS satellites transmit two types of ranging code signals:
rtk-gps.li1_1: L1 carrier (1575.42 MHz): Carries C/A code and P code
rtk-gps.li1_2: L2 carrier (1227.60 MHz): Only carries P code (encrypted as Y code)
rtk-gps.p1_2: Carrier wavelength: L1 is approximately 19.4cm, L2 is approximately 24.4cm. Carrier phase measurement accuracy is more than 100 times higher than pseudo-range code measurement accuracy.
rtk-gps.p2_1: Traditional GPS positioning calculates position by measuring the pseudo-range from satellite to receiver using triangulation. Pseudo-range = signal propagation time × speed of light.
rtk-gps.p2_2: However, pseudo-range measurement accuracy is limited (approximately 0.5-3 meters). Main error sources include:
rtk-gps.p3_1: Carrier phase measurement uses L1/L2 carrier phase information to calculate distance. The receiver measures the phase difference between the received carrier signal and the internal reference signal.
rtk-gps.p3_2: Carrier phase observation can be expressed as:
rtk-gps.p3_3: Where N is the integer ambiguity, the core unresolved parameter of carrier phase measurement.
rtk-gps.p4_1: Integer ambiguity N is the number of complete carrier cycles between receiver and satellite. Once N is accurately determined, very precise distance can be calculated using carrier phase.
rtk-gps.li4_1: Search method: Establish ambiguity candidate domain, search for optimal solution using least squares
rtk-gps.li4_2: LAMBDA: Least-squares Ambiguity Decorrelation Adjustment, the most commonly used method in industry
rtk-gps.li4_3: Kalman filtering: Dynamically estimate ambiguity and its changes
rtk-gps.p5_1: DGPS improves positioning accuracy by having a base station at a known position measure GPS errors and send these corrections to the rover station.
rtk-gps.p5_3: After the rover applies corrections, accuracy can be improved to 1-5 meters.
rtk-gps.p6_1: RTK goes a step further than DGPS by using carrier phase as the observation:
rtk-gps.li6_1: Base station: Precisely known position, continuously tracking all visible satellite carrier phases
rtk-gps.li6_2: Rover station: Simultaneously observes satellite carrier phases and receives base station correction data
rtk-gps.li6_3: Real-time correction: Real-time transmission of carrier phase corrections via radio link
rtk-gps.p6_2: The key to RTK is that base station and rover simultaneously observe the same satellites, errors are highly correlated, and through double-difference observation, most common errors can be eliminated.
rtk-gps.p7_1: RTK uses double-difference (DD) observations to eliminate systematic errors:
rtk-gps.li7_1: Single difference: Difference in observations of the same satellite between rover and base station, eliminates satellite clock error
rtk-gps.li7_2: Double difference: Difference in single differences between two satellites, eliminates receiver clock error
rtk-gps.p7_2: After double-difference processing, main remaining errors include:
rtk-gps.li7_3: Residual atmospheric delay (can be ignored when baseline is short, <5km)
rtk-gps.func1: Fixed known point, continuously tracking satellites and calculating correction data
rtk-gps.func2: PA-GS series, receiving correction data and calculating position in real-time
rtk-gps.func3: UHF radio or network, real-time transmission of correction data
rtk-gps.func4: Parameter settings, status monitoring, data recording
rtk-gps.li_uav1: High-precision aerial photography control point measurement
rtk-gps.summary: RTK technology achieves real-time centimeter-level positioning accuracy through three core elements: carrier phase measurement, real-time differential correction, and integer ambiguity resolution. PA-GS series RTK products combine multi-system fusion (GPS/GLONASS/BDS) and high update rate output, providing reliable solutions for surveying, precision agriculture, UAV and engineering control applications.
rtk-gps.contact: For more product information or technical consultation, please contact our sales team.
rtk-gps.cardTitle: RTK GPS Principles: Key Technologies for Centimeter-Level Positioning
rtk-gps.cardExcerpt: This article provides a detailed introduction to RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) positioning technology, including carrier phase measurement, differential correction, ambiguity resolution and other core technologies, as well as RTK system composition and application scenarios.
